title: Parenteral Nutrition
oneLiner: IV provision of macronutrients + micronutrients for patients with intestinal failure or gut inaccessible; high complication risk demands daily surveillance.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI):** Sudden fever ≥38.5°C, rigors, hypotension with a tunnelled CVC or PICC. *Killer finding: new hypotension (MAP <65) in a patient with an acute line — do not wait for blood cultures; start antibiotics after drawing cultures.*
- **Refeeding syndrome:** Hypophosphataemia (<0.32 mmol/L) + hypokalaemia + hypomagnesaemia within 72 hours of starting PN in a malnourished patient. *Killer finding: serum phosphate <0.32 mmol/L with ECG changes (low voltage, prolonged QT) or new arrhythmia — treat as emergency.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Patient receiving PN via central line
  - Fever, chills, rigors (line infection)
  - Abdominal distension, nausea (if bowel rest prescribed but patient still has symptoms)
  - Muscle cramps, weakness, palpitations (electrolyte disturbances)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythema, purulence, tenderness at line exit site
  - Tachycardia, hypotension (sepsis)
  - Poor peripheral perfusion, cold peripheries
  - Signs of fluid overload (peripheral oedema, raised JVP) if PN volume too high
  - In patients with short bowel: high stoma output, dehydration
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI) vs. other nosocomial sepsis (e.g., UTI, pneumonia). *Splitter: isolated fever with line in situ and no other localising features → CRBSI until proven otherwise.*
- **Common:** PN-related metabolic abnormalities (refeeding syndrome, hyperglycaemia, electrolyte disturbances). *Splitter: hypophosphataemia + history of recent PN start → refeeding syndrome; hyperglycaemia alone → overfeeding or stress response.*
- **Less likely:** Line thrombosis with or without pulmonary embolism (swollen arm, neck vein distension, dyspnoea). *Splitter: unilaterally swollen arm + line side → thrombosis; bilateral swelling → SVCO.*
- **Rare but deadly:** PN-related liver disease (cholestatic jaundice after weeks of PN). *Splitter: raised ALP and γGT with normal bilirubin early; conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia late.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Line site inspection and gentle palpation (do not manipulate hub)
  - ECG if any rhythm disturbance (check QT interval for refeeding)
  - Basic observations (NEWS2), temperature, BP
  - Urgent blood gas (pH, lactate, glucose, electrolyte screen)
  - For suspected line infection: **take two sets of peripheral blood cultures** and one set from the line lumen (if possible) – label clearly
- **Bloods:**
  - **Daily PN panel:** U&E, creatinine, phosphate, magnesium, calcium (corrected), LFTs, glucose, CRP — do every morning while patient on PN
  - **Pre-PN start (within 24h):** FBC, clotting, vitamin D, B12, folate, ferritin, selenium, zinc — baseline nutritional screen
  - **Suspect CRBSI:** blood cultures (peripheral + line), FBC, CRP, procalcitonin if available
  - **Suspect refeeding:** urgent phosphate, potassium, magnesium
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR if line placed recently (check tip position – SVC/RA junction) or suspected malposition
  - **Second-line:** Ultrasound/Doppler of limb if line thrombosis suspected; CT chest if mediastinal infection/sepsis source unclear
- **Special tests:**
  - *If line infection persists:* line tip culture on removal, guided by microbiology
  - *If cholestatic jaundice:* abdominal ultrasound to rule out gallstones/biliary obstruction before attributing to PN
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE malnutrition screening:** MUST score ≥2 → consider PN only if enteral route fails/contraindicated
  - **Refeeding syndrome criteria (NICE):** MUST score high + any of: BMI <16, unintentional weight loss >15% in 3–6 months, very little intake >10 days, low pre-existing K+/Mg2+/PO4 – start PN at 50% of target calories for 48h
  - **CRBSI diagnosis (IDSA):** positive culture from line > peripheral by ≥2h; or same organism from line and peripheral
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Pre-PN start: serum phosphate, potassium, magnesium** – to guide refeeding prevention. *Omission is a serious error – patient may be started on full rate PN and develop life-threatening hypophosphataemia.*
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - *Suspected CRBSI with haemodynamic instability:* do not wait – start empiric IV antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam [check local policy]) after cultures; remove line if confirmed sepsis and another central access available; if not, discuss with microbiology.
  - *Suspected refeeding syndrome (phosphate <0.32, K+ <3.0, Mg2+ <0.5 mmol/L):* **Stop PN** immediately. Give IV phosphate e.g., Phosphate Polyfusor 50 mmol over 12h (check local policy), IV K+ replacement (max rate 10 mmol/h via peripheral line, 20 mmol/h via central), IV Mg2+ (e.g., 10 mmol over 2h). Monitor ECG.
  - *Hypoglycaemia (rare but can occur if PN stopped abruptly):* restart IV glucose 10% at matching rate until new PN bag hung.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - *Daily bloods as above.*
  - *PN rate:* start at 50% of calculated target for first 24–48h if at high risk of refeeding; increase to full by day 3–4 if electrolytes stable.
  - *Line care:* change giving set every 24h, use chlorhexidine-impregnated dressings, no routine tubing disconnection.
  - *Fluid balance:* strict input/output chart; adjust PN volume if signs of overload.
  - *Blood glucose monitoring:* define sliding scale if hyperglycaemia (target 6–10 mmol/L); avoid hypoglycaemia. Insulin can be added to PN bag (prescribe via pharmacy).
  - *Nutritional assessment:* refer to dietitian for total energy/protein needs (25–35 kcal/kg/day, 1.2–1.5 g protein/kg).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Home PN (HPN) requires specialist team, tunnelled line (Hickman), rigorous training
  - Monitor for PN-associated liver disease: if bilirubin rising, consider reducing lipid emulsion or cycling PN (12h on, 12h off)
  - Prevent thrombotic complications: consider prophylactic low-dose warfarin (INR 1.2–1.5) or LMWH if prior thrombosis (discuss with haematology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** PN bag usually provides all fluid needs; if additional fluids needed, use 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s – avoid dextrose-containing bags unless prescribed
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless evidence of line thrombosis or bleeding
  - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM if bowel rest required; otherwise, allow oral intake as tolerated (do not use “NBM” unnecessarily)
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly observations (NEWS2) – increase to 1-hourly if unstable or high lactate
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Insulin added to PN bag (hyperglycaemia):* starting dose 0.1-0.2 units per gram of carbohydrate in bag – [check local policy / pharmacy]. Do not prescribe as separate SC insulin until PN rate stable.
  - *IV phosphate replacement:* Phosphate Polyfusor 50 mmol over 12h (if PO4 <0.32); check K+ and Ca2+ before and during infusion
  - *IV potassium replacement:* 40–60 mmol in 1L 0.9% saline over 4–6h (max 10 mmol/h peripheral, 20 mmol/h central)
  - *IV magnesium replacement:* 10–20 mmol magnesium sulfate over 2–4h (max 50 mmol/day)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *For CRBSI empiric cover:* vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV 12-hourly (check levels) + piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6-hourly (check local policy)
  - *For line salvage in uncomplicated CRBSI:* antibiotic lock (e.g., vancomycin 5 mg/mL) – discuss with microbiology
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - *Do not give PN peripherally (except short-term, low-osmolarity solutions) – can cause phlebitis*
  - *Do not give IV antibiotics through the same lumen as PN unless designated triple-lumen catheter and one lumen dedicated*
  - *Dobutamine / other inotropes – must have dedicated lumen, never Y-site with PN due to incompatibility*
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - *Renal impairment:* reduce PN volume and potassium; use lower protein intake (0.8–1.0 g/kg if not on dialysis)
  - *Hepatic impairment:* use lower lipid dose (maximum 1 g/kg/day) and consider branched-chain amino acid formulations (specialist only)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting PN at full rate in a malnourished patient** – triggers refeeding syndrome with fatal hypophosphataemia/arrhythmia. *The single most harmful mistake. Always check baseline phosphate, K+, Mg2+ and use 50% rate for first 48h if high risk.*
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Refeeding syndrome** – hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, fluid overload, respiratory failure
  - **Hyperglycaemia** – osmotic diuresis, hyperosmolar state, infection risk
  - **Hypoglycaemia** – if PN stopped abruptly
  - **Catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI)** – sepsis, endocarditis
  - **Line thrombosis** – SVC syndrome, PE
  - **Pneumothorax / haemothorax** – during insertion
- **Chronic:**
  - **PN-associated liver disease (PNALD)** – cholestasis, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis (especially with long-term PN)
  - **Metabolic bone disease** – osteomalacia, fragility fractures
  - **Micronutrient deficiencies** – zinc, selenium, vitamin D, essential fatty acids (if not supplemented in bag)
  - **Central vein stenosis/thrombosis** – limits future access
sections.Prognosis:
- Short-term PN (e.g., post-surgery): usually weaned when gut function returns; mortality <5% from complications.
- Long-term PN (e.g., short bowel syndrome): 1–5 year survival ~80–90% with dedicated HPN teams; death often from CRBSI or PN-liver disease.
- Development of cholestatic jaundice (bilirubin >50 µmol/L) predicts liver failure within 1–2 years if not reversed.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check phosphate, K+, Mg2+ before starting PN** and again 6h after first bag – this is the single action that prevents refeeding deaths.
- **Write daily PN prescription as a separate order** including rate, insulin need, and any extra electrolytes – never delegate to “TPN as per bag label” without review.
- **Label the line hub clearly** – one lumen for PN, one for blood sampling, no other Y-site infusions without pharmacy compatibility check.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never prescribe PN without a dedicated central line** – peripheral PN causes thrombophlebitis and extravasation injury.
- **Do not stop PN suddenly** – causes rebound hypoglycaemia; wean over 2h or run 10% dextrose equally.
- **Avoid giving additional diuretics or excessive free water** – PN is hypertonic and patients already at risk of fluid overload; strict input/output chart is mandatory.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new fever >38°C, rigors, chills; sudden swelling/redness at line site; shortness of breath or chest pain; inability to keep the PN bag cold or contamination.
- **Follow-up:** HPN nurse specialist within 24–48h; dietitian and gastroenterology clinic within 2 weeks; bloods weekly (U&E, LFT, Mg, PO4, glucose) while on HPN.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar or Consultant) immediately for:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or ≥3 in one parameter)
  - Serum phosphate <0.3 mmol/L despite replacement; new arrhythmia; seizure
  - Suspected CRBSI with hypotension (MAP <65) – escalate to ICU/CCOT
  - Hyperglycaemia >20 mmol/L or hypoglycaemia <3.0 mmol/L
  - New onset jaundice (bilirubin > twice upper limit of normal)
  - Complete occlusion of central line with no alternative access

rawMarkdown:
## Parenteral Nutrition
**One-liner:** IV provision of macronutrients + micronutrients for patients with intestinal failure or gut inaccessible; high complication risk demands daily surveillance.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI):** Sudden fever ≥38.5°C, rigors, hypotension with a tunnelled CVC or PICC. *Killer finding: new hypotension (MAP <65) in a patient with an acute line — do not wait for blood cultures; start antibiotics after drawing cultures.*
- **Refeeding syndrome:** Hypophosphataemia (<0.32 mmol/L) + hypokalaemia + hypomagnesaemia within 72 hours of starting PN in a malnourished patient. *Killer finding: serum phosphate <0.32 mmol/L with ECG changes (low voltage, prolonged QT) or new arrhythmia — treat as emergency.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Patient receiving PN via central line
  - Fever, chills, rigors (line infection)
  - Abdominal distension, nausea (if bowel rest prescribed but patient still has symptoms)
  - Muscle cramps, weakness, palpitations (electrolyte disturbances)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythema, purulence, tenderness at line exit site
  - Tachycardia, hypotension (sepsis)
  - Poor peripheral perfusion, cold peripheries
  - Signs of fluid overload (peripheral oedema, raised JVP) if PN volume too high
  - In patients with short bowel: high stoma output, dehydration

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI) vs. other nosocomial sepsis (e.g., UTI, pneumonia). *Splitter: isolated fever with line in situ and no other localising features → CRBSI until proven otherwise.*
- **Common:** PN-related metabolic abnormalities (refeeding syndrome, hyperglycaemia, electrolyte disturbances). *Splitter: hypophosphataemia + history of recent PN start → refeeding syndrome; hyperglycaemia alone → overfeeding or stress response.*
- **Less likely:** Line thrombosis with or without pulmonary embolism (swollen arm, neck vein distension, dyspnoea). *Splitter: unilaterally swollen arm + line side → thrombosis; bilateral swelling → SVCO.*
- **Rare but deadly:** PN-related liver disease (cholestatic jaundice after weeks of PN). *Splitter: raised ALP and γGT with normal bilirubin early; conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia late.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Line site inspection and gentle palpation (do not manipulate hub)
  - ECG if any rhythm disturbance (check QT interval for refeeding)
  - Basic observations (NEWS2), temperature, BP
  - Urgent blood gas (pH, lactate, glucose, electrolyte screen)
  - For suspected line infection: **take two sets of peripheral blood cultures** and one set from the line lumen (if possible) – label clearly
- **Bloods:**
  - **Daily PN panel:** U&E, creatinine, phosphate, magnesium, calcium (corrected), LFTs, glucose, CRP — do every morning while patient on PN
  - **Pre-PN start (within 24h):** FBC, clotting, vitamin D, B12, folate, ferritin, selenium, zinc — baseline nutritional screen
  - **Suspect CRBSI:** blood cultures (peripheral + line), FBC, CRP, procalcitonin if available
  - **Suspect refeeding:** urgent phosphate, potassium, magnesium
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR if line placed recently (check tip position – SVC/RA junction) or suspected malposition
  - **Second-line:** Ultrasound/Doppler of limb if line thrombosis suspected; CT chest if mediastinal infection/sepsis source unclear
- **Special tests:**
  - *If line infection persists:* line tip culture on removal, guided by microbiology
  - *If cholestatic jaundice:* abdominal ultrasound to rule out gallstones/biliary obstruction before attributing to PN
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE malnutrition screening:** MUST score ≥2 → consider PN only if enteral route fails/contraindicated
  - **Refeeding syndrome criteria (NICE):** MUST score high + any of: BMI <16, unintentional weight loss >15% in 3–6 months, very little intake >10 days, low pre-existing K+/Mg2+/PO4 – start PN at 50% of target calories for 48h
  - **CRBSI diagnosis (IDSA):** positive culture from line > peripheral by ≥2h; or same organism from line and peripheral
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Pre-PN start: serum phosphate, potassium, magnesium** – to guide refeeding prevention. *Omission is a serious error – patient may be started on full rate PN and develop life-threatening hypophosphataemia.*

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - *Suspected CRBSI with haemodynamic instability:* do not wait – start empiric IV antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam [check local policy]) after cultures; remove line if confirmed sepsis and another central access available; if not, discuss with microbiology.
  - *Suspected refeeding syndrome (phosphate <0.32, K+ <3.0, Mg2+ <0.5 mmol/L):* **Stop PN** immediately. Give IV phosphate e.g., Phosphate Polyfusor 50 mmol over 12h (check local policy), IV K+ replacement (max rate 10 mmol/h via peripheral line, 20 mmol/h via central), IV Mg2+ (e.g., 10 mmol over 2h). Monitor ECG.
  - *Hypoglycaemia (rare but can occur if PN stopped abruptly):* restart IV glucose 10% at matching rate until new PN bag hung.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - *Daily bloods as above.*
  - *PN rate:* start at 50% of calculated target for first 24–48h if at high risk of refeeding; increase to full by day 3–4 if electrolytes stable.
  - *Line care:* change giving set every 24h, use chlorhexidine-impregnated dressings, no routine tubing disconnection.
  - *Fluid balance:* strict input/output chart; adjust PN volume if signs of overload.
  - *Blood glucose monitoring:* define sliding scale if hyperglycaemia (target 6–10 mmol/L); avoid hypoglycaemia. Insulin can be added to PN bag (prescribe via pharmacy).
  - *Nutritional assessment:* refer to dietitian for total energy/protein needs (25–35 kcal/kg/day, 1.2–1.5 g protein/kg).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Home PN (HPN) requires specialist team, tunnelled line (Hickman), rigorous training
  - Monitor for PN-associated liver disease: if bilirubin rising, consider reducing lipid emulsion or cycling PN (12h on, 12h off)
  - Prevent thrombotic complications: consider prophylactic low-dose warfarin (INR 1.2–1.5) or LMWH if prior thrombosis (discuss with haematology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** PN bag usually provides all fluid needs; if additional fluids needed, use 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s – avoid dextrose-containing bags unless prescribed
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless evidence of line thrombosis or bleeding
  - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM if bowel rest required; otherwise, allow oral intake as tolerated (do not use “NBM” unnecessarily)
  - **Observation frequency:** 4-hourly observations (NEWS2) – increase to 1-hourly if unstable or high lactate

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Insulin added to PN bag (hyperglycaemia):* starting dose 0.1-0.2 units per gram of carbohydrate in bag – [check local policy / pharmacy]. Do not prescribe as separate SC insulin until PN rate stable.
  - *IV phosphate replacement:* Phosphate Polyfusor 50 mmol over 12h (if PO4 <0.32); check K+ and Ca2+ before and during infusion
  - *IV potassium replacement:* 40–60 mmol in 1L 0.9% saline over 4–6h (max 10 mmol/h peripheral, 20 mmol/h central)
  - *IV magnesium replacement:* 10–20 mmol magnesium sulfate over 2–4h (max 50 mmol/day)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *For CRBSI empiric cover:* vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV 12-hourly (check levels) + piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV 6-hourly (check local policy)
  - *For line salvage in uncomplicated CRBSI:* antibiotic lock (e.g., vancomycin 5 mg/mL) – discuss with microbiology
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - *Do not give PN peripherally (except short-term, low-osmolarity solutions) – can cause phlebitis*
  - *Do not give IV antibiotics through the same lumen as PN unless designated triple-lumen catheter and one lumen dedicated*
  - *Dobutamine / other inotropes – must have dedicated lumen, never Y-site with PN due to incompatibility*
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - *Renal impairment:* reduce PN volume and potassium; use lower protein intake (0.8–1.0 g/kg if not on dialysis)
  - *Hepatic impairment:* use lower lipid dose (maximum 1 g/kg/day) and consider branched-chain amino acid formulations (specialist only)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting PN at full rate in a malnourished patient** – triggers refeeding syndrome with fatal hypophosphataemia/arrhythmia. *The single most harmful mistake. Always check baseline phosphate, K+, Mg2+ and use 50% rate for first 48h if high risk.*

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Refeeding syndrome** – hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, fluid overload, respiratory failure
  - **Hyperglycaemia** – osmotic diuresis, hyperosmolar state, infection risk
  - **Hypoglycaemia** – if PN stopped abruptly
  - **Catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI)** – sepsis, endocarditis
  - **Line thrombosis** – SVC syndrome, PE
  - **Pneumothorax / haemothorax** – during insertion
- **Chronic:**
  - **PN-associated liver disease (PNALD)** – cholestasis, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis (especially with long-term PN)
  - **Metabolic bone disease** – osteomalacia, fragility fractures
  - **Micronutrient deficiencies** – zinc, selenium, vitamin D, essential fatty acids (if not supplemented in bag)
  - **Central vein stenosis/thrombosis** – limits future access

### Prognosis
- Short-term PN (e.g., post-surgery): usually weaned when gut function returns; mortality <5% from complications.
- Long-term PN (e.g., short bowel syndrome): 1–5 year survival ~80–90% with dedicated HPN teams; death often from CRBSI or PN-liver disease.
- Development of cholestatic jaundice (bilirubin >50 µmol/L) predicts liver failure within 1–2 years if not reversed.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check phosphate, K+, Mg2+ before starting PN** and again 6h after first bag – this is the single action that prevents refeeding deaths.
- **Write daily PN prescription as a separate order** including rate, insulin need, and any extra electrolytes – never delegate to “TPN as per bag label” without review.
- **Label the line hub clearly** – one lumen for PN, one for blood sampling, no other Y-site infusions without pharmacy compatibility check.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never prescribe PN without a dedicated central line** – peripheral PN causes thrombophlebitis and extravasation injury.
- **Do not stop PN suddenly** – causes rebound hypoglycaemia; wean over 2h or run 10% dextrose equally.
- **Avoid giving additional diuretics or excessive free water** – PN is hypertonic and patients already at risk of fluid overload; strict input/output chart is mandatory.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new fever >38°C, rigors, chills; sudden swelling/redness at line site; shortness of breath or chest pain; inability to keep the PN bag cold or contamination.
- **Follow-up:** HPN nurse specialist within 24–48h; dietitian and gastroenterology clinic within 2 weeks; bloods weekly (U&E, LFT, Mg, PO4, glucose) while on HPN.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar or Consultant) immediately for:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or ≥3 in one parameter)
  - Serum phosphate <0.3 mmol/L despite replacement; new arrhythmia; seizure
  - Suspected CRBSI with hypotension (MAP <65) – escalate to ICU/CCOT
  - Hyperglycaemia >20 mmol/L or hypoglycaemia <3.0 mmol/L
  - New onset jaundice (bilirubin > twice upper limit of normal)
  - Complete occlusion of central line with no alternative access
